item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Do it!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I'll keep an eye out for that ideal tale as I continue the wikiwalk though.

We found it, Conwell: Angel of Red and White. I'll probably update the crosslink at some stage, unless anyone tells me why I shouldn't.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Updated the crosslink to It Just Shattered. I'll keep an eye out for that ideal tale as I continue the wikiwalk though.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
This is the logical link, and I'm fine with it - although if you could find the tale where Green first meets Orange (in the Arctic? am I making that up?) it might be even better. Particularly as this incident is intended to occur prior to The Cool War, so linking to Green's past, rather than his future, makes more sense.

If that's not possible (or if I'm referring to a tale that doesn't exist) then a slight variation I might suggest is to link directly to It Just Shattered, rather than to the Cool War hub. The hub doesn't give any indication that Agent Green is involved in the story, and neither does the first tale. It Just Shattered is only the second tale in the series, and a perfectly fine jumping-on point for readers - they can always read Wowee afterwards - and it introduces Agent Green from almost the first line.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

There is a chance that readers stumbling upon this SCP article may like character of Agent Green, but not know of his origins in The Cool War, and not have read the madness that is that tale series. Seems like making a link from the first mention of the Agent to the Cool War Hub would work as an easy, and rather non-invasive crosslink. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

Interviewed: Agent ██████ Green
Interviewer: Assistant-Director Griffiths, Head of Site-15 Disciplinary Committee
Date: 28 June 2013

To this:

Interviewed: Agent ██████ Green
Interviewer: Assistant-Director Griffiths, Head of Site-15 Disciplinary Committee
Date: 28 June 2013

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome, thanks

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hi Gardeford - yes, the title is an excerpt from the lyrics to the song "A Wonderful Guy" from the musical South Pacific.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the title here a reference to something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm from a kingdom level above human. What does that yield? A hokey smile that damns an entire nation.

Just saying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here. I was surprised it turned out to be a smiley face.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Show Response
- Hide Response

There is an SCP contained anomaly here. There are the related documents. You are downvoting the SCP because it isn't complete, but complaining that the related documents are too tale-like. The SCP couldn't be posted as part of the tale, because of the reason above. So what exactly do you expect here? It seems really "damned if you, damned if you don't", to me.

I upvoted the "related documents" tale, and they are, well, a tale. I thought this anomaly works really well in that format and is a great example of how you can feature a new anomaly in a tale. Unlike the SCP itself, the "related documents" stand on their own (though I don't hold tales to that requirement anyways) and do not need a corresponding SCP.

What I'm saying is: "if your skip doesn't work in SCP format, change the format and make it a tale." You seem to think I'm saying: "if your skip doesn't work in SCP format, keep the SCP format but make it a tale." Nope.

Indeed, the "Figure and Ground" related documents are a GREAT example of a well-done tale written as a collection of several supporting documents that could potentially be featured in an SCP, but work better as a tale.

In my opinion, as it is currently written, the SCP does not stand on its own. Without the tale, the SCP falls flat. And the "Incident Log" used was probably the most boring part of the entire saga, and adds nothing to the SCP. The "Incident Log" is a superfluous story… and yet, when looking at the SCP on its own, the SCP seems to exist just to tell it.

I hold SCPs to a high standard. They are to be neat and clinical, paint an overall singular picture, with as much brevity and as little superfluous information as possible. I always read SCPs like I am a Level 4 researcher reading a summary of a much larger series of documents. Whatever assistant who created the skip brief has carefully chosen the most important documents to paint the best picture of the SCP as possible. In this case, I would ask that person, "Why did you choose this incident log, and not the diary entries which are evidence towards who created it and why?" I understand doing something from a narrative sense, but I believe that it falls flat in that aspect.

Anyways, I am done answering questions on the subject. I don't want this to look like an argument or anything. So if you or the author or anyone else have more questions, feel free to message me. :-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wouldn't recommend posting a "tale" in skip format. It could be done, but you could post a "tale" as a series of related documents, as psul successfully did in his accompanying tale, "Figure and Ground."

Then what does:

SCPs like that should be tales.

Actually mean?

There is an SCP contained anomaly here. There are the related documents. You are downvoting the SCP because it isn't complete, but complaining that the related documents are too tale-like. The SCP couldn't be posted as part of the tale, because of the reason above. So what exactly do you expect here? It seems really "damned if you, damned if you don't", to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've summarized responses to the above three posts herein.

+ Long response
- Hide

Posting something labeled as a 'tale" in SCP format also seems to attract downvotes for violating convention…

I wouldn't recommend posting a "tale" in skip format. It could be done, but you could post a "tale" as a series of related documents, as psul successfully did in his accompanying tale, "Figure and Ground."

So I assume you think a fair good number of skips should be tales? Would you downvote them for that reason?

When I come across one like that, yes, I think I do.

That sounds really restricting (almost elitist but I'm assuming you're not saying this with elitist intentions). We can't ever try something new or buck a format because we might as well go write it on some other site?

I'm not trying to sound elitist at all… and I am a sucker for good format-screws, so I don't mind people turning the format on its head. But the format is there for a reason. We're describing objects or places or phenomenon, and in doing so, telling a story. Not saying, "here's an object, and here's a story to go with it" which is what I consider "Post-Incident Interview SCP-2162-A/Green/01" to be. And the more I think about it, the more I think that's why I don't like it. It's not a recovery log, or an exploration log, it does almost nothing to tell us more about the skip itself. It's just there, as an added story. Now I did ready Figure and Ground, the accompaniment, and I liked it, and i think there's some stuff in there that DOES add some more to the skip, but this Incident log isn't it.

Thanks for your thoughts, Abettik. As with Gaffney, I'd be interested to hear further from you if you read the Tale as well. Do you think that it would be enough for you, that it would have a more interesting story overall, if presented as a single article? Or is your view that the whole thing should be a tale?

I did read it, and I did respond to it. The answers to these questions are there.

To take some famous/favourite examples, I would say that each of SCP-2188 and SCP-093 tell stories through logs. Are those the type of articles you're referring to? What about SCP-087? I think that also tells a story (albeit one that is very limited in range), but I'm not sure what side of the line it falls.

I have never read 2188, and I never could get through 093. I tried 093 and gave up. But for 087, those are exploration logs, and therefore, by definition, they are telling us more about the skip itself. I think your incident log here feels tacked on to me… it doesn't add anything to the skip itself. It doesn't tell us where it is or where it came from. It tells us why to fear it, but anyone with two brain cells would have known that already. It tells us how to detect it, but again, you already mention a detection method in your description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thought this looked familiar, I read these in chat! I don't mind the scip and the supplements being separate, it works for me. Liked it then and liked it now.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So did I. I thought the giant dick was better, actually, pointing up the immaturity of the anartist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
minmin is correct - the title is purely part of an Easter Egg spread across the two pages.

+ Annoying list of disconnected hints
- Can't say I didn't warn you

big money on behalf of a rich widow in Beverly Hills

Who cares if the old lady … doesn't know what bromidic means. Just give her Mitzi Gaynor …

causing a diversion using tanks of liquid bromine

I mean, I get the bromine shipped at unnecessary risk

so if a member wants some old actress' grinning mug to float around her lounge

SCP-2162 moves at a constant rate of 0.25km/hr … along longitude 118.25°W

On 24 August 2015, the construct reached latitude 8.65°S and changed course, turning 90° and travelling West

Current calculations predict late 2021 as the likely date of arrival of SCP-2162 in densely populated areas


My headcanon 3rd Act to this story is called "A wonderful guy". It starts with a conversation between Navarro and Green about being thrown off the case:

"Grounded?"
Navarro nodded back.
"Figures."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for your thoughts, Abettik. As with Gaffney, I'd be interested to hear further from you if you read the Tale as well. Do you think that it would be enough for you, that it would have a more interesting story overall, if presented as a single article? Or is your view that the whole thing should be a tale?

I agree with you that one of the interesting things about the site is the restriction imposed by the SCP format. But I'm not sure if I'm misinterpreting your comment:

I almost never like articles whose sole purpose is to tell a story in the retrieval log/interview log/exploration log.

because I'm not sure where you draw the line. To take some famous/favourite examples, I would say that each of SCP-2188 and SCP-093 tell stories through logs. Are those the type of articles you're referring to? What about SCP-087? I think that also tells a story (albeit one that is very limited in range), but I'm not sure what side of the line it falls.

I'm not asking to be confrontational, quite the opposite. I am genuinely interested in how readers approach the site, and what they enjoy. It won't necessarily change how I write, but at least I'll understand when I'm pushing against the boundaries.

Edit: Also, I should comment on "gave me the nudge immediately to realizing this was AWCY". If you read the Tale, you'll see that this is not quite how the anomaly was created. However it is true on a deeper level - part of the inspiration for this article was a post somewhere suggesting stories about GoIs interacting between themselves, and I thought "Why doesn't MC&D commission artwork from AWCY?".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://www.metrolyrics.com/a-wonderful-guy-lyrics-south-pacific.html

First google result I got. I… guess? it fits?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. Can't believe nobody's thought of a killer smiley face before and the way it was done here was good. Upvoted for smiley face of death.

EDIT: I don't understand the title of this SCP though. Care to explain?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The effect is interesting, and the form (once I figured out what it was, thanks, Famine Thamine) makes it intriguing, but I agree with the above: I want to know why this was made. That, and

South. South, away from the invisible wave of death. And towards the twelve thousand citizens of Malibu, who had no clue what was coming.

Sounds awfully poetic and dramatic for an interview. If nothing else, it doesn't fit Green's characterization up to that point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh thank god. From the comment at the end, I thought it was a giant dick. D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCPs like that should be tales.

So I assume you think a fair good number of skips should be tales? Would you downvote them for that reason?

If you don't like the format, you are free to write elsewhere, or write a tale, or add supplemental documents, or a bibliography, and see how well they are received.

That sounds really restricting (almost elitist but I'm assuming you're not saying this with elitist intentions). We can't ever try something new or buck a format because we might as well go write it on some other site?

item.title: Title?
item.content:encoded: It occurs to me that I still am not sure that I understand the title. "Blueberry pie" isn't remotely abnormal, but the idiom is normally "apple pie", right? So this is something quotidian, a smiley face, in a bizarre idiom - giant killer aphysical gas cloud. Is that it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCPs like that should be tales.

Posting something labeled as a 'tale" in SCP format also seems to attract downvotes for violating convention…

That's the challenge isn't it?

Sure, and formal style restrictions often generate creativity. Occasionally though, it might be worth considering a work in a larger context.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1

I got the :) image right away from the description. The "You get a feeling when something is man-made," line, plus the shape, gave me the nudge immediately to realizing this was AWCY. Kudos for not having to say "Are you cool yet??!!" or some stupid thing in order to hammer that point in, but I still don't like it as a standalone article, for several reasons.

+ Show whatever
- Hide whatever

I'm not a big fan of the hook here. There's the funny bit of it being a smiley face, and the slightly interesting story, but it's not enough for me. As others have said, maybe if we had some hints as to WHY or HOW this thing was created, it might be more interesting. But to me I find it a bit boring.

Additionally, I almost never like articles whose sole purpose is to tell a story in the retrieval log/interview log/exploration log. SCPs like that should be tales. This one even moreso, because it then links to a tale (which I haven't read yet), driving that point home like a punch in the gut.

I will respond to some one other comment here, so as not to clog the board.

sirpudding:
I think that unfortunately this attitude is very limiting to our creativity on this site. In-universe an SCP document would be only the very top page of a thick file of supporting documentation, yet it is nearly impossible to write an SCP that reveals itself through a series of documents because of the way that people vote on them as separate things. Many people have said that I should have written these things for SCP-2140 but I have no doubt that a lengthy statistical study of people's biographies with 2140 clearance compared to controls would be downvoted into oblivion by the same people who are asking for it.

That's the challenge isn't it? There are literally thousands of other creative writing sites on the internet, and there are also literally thousands of SCPs that stand very well on their own. If you don't like the format, you are free to write elsewhere, or write a tale, or add supplemental documents, or a bibliography, and see how well they are received. IMO, you never know which skip someone is going to randomly click on, and "there is no canon," so every SCP does need to stand more or less on its own.

In my mind, the best skips have containment procedures, descriptions, interview logs/test logs/recover logs/letters and a myriad of other supplemental materials all working together in harmony to show an interesting picture. That can be a story, emotional or creepy or otherwise. That can be just an image of an interesting phenomenon. It can be a mystery to unravel, some kernel of truth the reader has to put together. I'm not saying this SCP doesn't try to do that; it does, but it tries and fails. It really seems like it was meant to be a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally didn't feel that it was every "too long", and wouldn't mind if "Figure and Ground" was folded back in, or if they remained separate. Though honestly, "If Dragnet was written by Raymond Chandler and about the Foundation" would probably have me upvoting in any form, providing you didn't ruin it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An SCP article should work as a standalone document.

I think that unfortunately this attitude is very limiting to our creativity on this site. In-universe an SCP document would be only the very top page of a thick file of supporting documentation, yet it is nearly impossible to write an SCP that reveals itself through a series of documents because of the way that people vote on them as separate things. Many people have said that I should have written these things for SCP-2140 but I have no doubt that a lengthy statistical study of people's biographies with 2140 clearance compared to controls would be downvoted into oblivion by the same people who are asking for it.

I had to create a fairly sophisticated work-around to make SCP-2111 a single work rather than five separate ones. Really though, it is only this bizarre artifact of voting that required this.

Why should a story have to fit on one page to be considered a single work? We don't expect this of written fiction in print media. Author intent should count for something here. In this case these two peices are clearly connected as a larger work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not as of yet, but that shouldn't matter, in my opinion. An SCP article should work as a standalone document.

I agree with that. In making the decision to split this in two, I had to have the confidence that the SCP part could work on its own.

Originally this and the Tale/Supplement linked to it were part of a single SCP article (other than the bookend text to the tale). Feedback was that putting it together made the whole thing too long, but no-one could work out what to cut. Splitting it is therefore a compromise (albeit one that I stand behind) rather than a deliberate decision to obscure the "interesting parts".

If you do read the Tale, Gaffney, let me know your thoughts on re-ordering/recombining. Part of the reason for posting was to get a discussion like this going!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not as of yet, but that shouldn't matter, in my opinion. An SCP article should work as a standalone document. This does not.

If the only interesting parts are obscured so that they can be put into a tale or other article, then the SCP article is not itself an interesting read. If the interesting information cannot be conveyed in the format of an SCP document, the author should either 1) work on it more so that it can be, or 2) not write it as an SCP article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I daresay I was lukewarm to this SCP as a whole, but I upvoted when I realised an agent actually released substances to make it coloured. It's all good thinking in the long run.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not a penis…

The funny thing is, Randomini initially pictured it the same way as Deacon_Blues, so I can see how that misconception arose. "Convex in the direction of travel" is the key descriptive line that perhaps isn't translating to mental images.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks Famine. Don't rush on my account - if this can't survive on its own, it doesn't deserve your hard work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
image
– hide block

item.title: Upvote.
item.content:encoded:
At first I wasn't sure what exactly is happening then when I finally got it I couldn't stop laughing.
A gigantic smiley face of death. Nice. Very nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Basically, how it kills people is totally clear, but why it was created, which is more interesting, imo, is never explained.

Did you read "Figure and Ground"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not a penis…

+ show block
– hide block


It's a smiley face.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In this case, I see the value of a visual - because what's been described here is a giant, crudely-drawn teenager's depiction of a penis. Sure, it's a titanic penis that replaces the normal atmosphere in its area with pure nitrogen, even supplies of air in tanks, but it is what it is - a sort of My First Anart thing. If the kid weren't an anartist, he'd be drawing these penises on posters and bathroom walls and such.

That actually kind of adds to the skip for me, the fact that it is indeed so very crude. We've been shown bad anart before, but it always wanted to say something, where this is anartistic graffiti - and crude, childish graffiti at that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Killbot" is a reference to Futurama, and was just me being a bit glib. Basically, it's a thing that kills people for no discernible reason1. The bit about the reveal was similarly me being glib. Basically, I expected there to be some sort of revelation at the end of the interview that would change the entire context for the piece.

I think that you're overestimating the power of visuals on an article. If you look at articles that have memorable pictures, such as 106 or 1867, you'll see that they compliment articles that already have fundamentally interesting ideas. Relying on a picture to carry an article that doesn't have much in the way of interesting concepts is not going to make the article interesting.

Footnotes
1. I get that it's an effect of it moving around and effectively suffocating people, but I don't really get the reasoning behind its creation. Basically, how it kills people is totally clear, but why it was created, which is more interesting, imo, is never explained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi Gaffney - sorry you didn't enjoy it, but I really appreciate you telling me why. And no need to apologise for the puns - I thought they were a gas.

I'm particularly sorry that you didn't get any striking images from the article. It might be that adding a picture would help - probably in a second collapsible immediately after the interview. That might provide slightly more payoff too, if that's what you're missing - I agree with you that this needs to stand on its own, despite the larger story being in the tale.

I don't understand your references to "killbot" (and the accompanying reveal you expected) - it would be great if you could explain what you meant by that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh huh, Agent Green. That's kind of interesting, I guess.

This work is really insubstantial1. There's whiffs of something interesting2, but it feels like its all obscured in favor of a payoff that never comes. The object itself is fairly humdrum; it is a killbot made of nitrogen. Potentially interesting questions, such as who made it, why its made of nitrogen, what purpose it's supposed to serve, etc. are addressed, as far as I can tell, in the article.

The bulk of the article is Agent Green's account of initial containment, which fails to give any meaningful characterization, new information, or even particularly striking images. At the end, I was left waiting for a last-line reveal of "and tehn agent grene was kilbot," but nothing ever happened. Maybe the tale adds some wonderful context that makes reading this worthwhile, but a mainlist article should be able to stand on its own. -1

Footnotes
1. Sorry
2. Sorry again

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a story about rendering visible those things that are invisible. Appropriately, Faminepulse has promised a picture of the anomaly if this survives long enough. Until then, you'll just have to grin and bear it :)

Attributions: Very many thanks to:

  • Randomini for letting me use Agent Green. My headcanon is that this is set a little before The Cool War.
  • TwistedGears for letting me use Agent Navarro, who turns up more in the supplemental tale.

It is a privilege to be allowed to take such great characters out for a spin, and I really appreciated your feedback and guidance on the first draft of this.

Acknowledgements: Thanks also to everyone who reviewed this on the forums and chat. Decibelles, WrongJohnSilver, Dr Hood and Dr Solo. And a particular thanks to sirpudding, who actually went hiking in the State Park in which this SCP is set, and took a whole pile of photos there. Sorry I haven't been able to use them yet!

The story continues in the tale linked at the end of the draft.
